evidence_quotes.quote: private servers of MMO or RTS games in the past

paragraphs.text: I fully support the digital fairness act in the sense of preserving the products a consumer buys/licenses. In the world of buying products it is very rare that someone would be able to at any point in time destroy what a person paid for, and so it is very unfortunate that this "common sense" does not apply to digital products. In the case of games as digital products as an active game developer with experience with implementing and maintaining servers and all the back-end infrastructure, I know that running the back-end for most games on question for much longer period of time would cost very little ( you could say "pennies"), or allowing enthusiasts access to the binaries and configuration so that they are able to host their own servers which would allow the apps/games to run indefinitely would be extremely easy (as was demonstrated with some apps already, like private servers of MMO or RTS games in the past). However that point has already been discussed in great detail. I personally would rather bring attention to the loss of art that happens anytime a digital game is "destroyed" this way. As a society for millenia we have tried to preserve any form of worthwhile art, so that future generations have some source of solace and inspiration, and a feeling of continuation to draw from. By letting games die we allow countless marvels of music, design, paintings and animation to be destroyed. This I find the greatest offense, one which I don't think should be allowed to continue any longer. Thus I implore the commity to support the digital fairness act and prevent this loss from happening again!
